text: The surgeon focuses on dissecting around the deep ring, incising the peritoneum to achieve a circumferential transection of the hernia neck at the deep ring level. The distal sac is successfully transected and dissociated from the peritoneum.

The surgeon performs medial dissection in the parietal compartment towards the pubis, followed by lateral dissection in the visceral compartment, aiming to elevate areolar tissue fibers against the lateral sidewall. An intentional hole at the deep ring is encountered, and careful dissection is performed around it to secure cord elements, blood vessels, and areolar tissue against the sidewall, minimizing injury risk.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and pulls on the obliterated umbilical artery while parietalizing the cord elements. The peritoneum is grabbed to establish a landing zone for the mesh. A small amount of preperitoneal fat is visible in the obturator space, which is tidied up to ensure a sufficient dissection for placing the extra large mesh.

The surgeon holds the obliterated umbilical artery as a marker for adequate parietalization of the vas, then incises and cuts through the artery to ensure the mesh lays flat. The hernia sac remains in the canal without manipulation to avoid inducing hematomas. An extra large mid-weight mesh is used to cover the defect, which is small with a large penetrating sac.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure by closing the primary transection of the peritoneum. The fenestration related to the abandonment of the sac is also closed.
